course_description: Every day we make decisions by relying on our personal theories about how things are supposed to work. But our reliance on these rules is paid for at the cost of accuracy. We tend to see what we expect to see and believe what we're told. One goal of this course is to figure out how everyday decision making can be improved.
timetable:
Timetables are available on the UQ Public Timetable.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims: To develop an in-depth and integrative understanding of how approaching judgment and decision making from a psychological perspective improves our understanding of human behaviour.

learning_outcomes.description: Gain a thorough understanding of the fundamental concepts and theories in the field of judgement and decision making, including biases and heuristics, dual-process theories, non-human feats of intelligence, reasoning and problem solving, insight and metacognition, and decision making under uncertainty.

Analyse and apply these theories to real-world situations, such as high-stakes decision making in fields like medicine, law, and education, using large language models like ChatGPT to improve decision-making abilities and critically evaluate decision-making processes.

Enhance their ability to identify and avoid common biases and heuristics in their own decision-making by working with AI chatbots and strategies developed in ChatGPT assignments, and develop and implement effective decision-making strategies.

Evaluate and analyse decision-making strategies, including group decision making and nudging, and understand the impact of brain training, transfer, and mental representations on expertise, using critical thinking and feedback skills developed in ChatGPT assignments.

Effectively communicate their understanding of judgement and decision making to others, using the communication skills developed in the ChatGPT assignments and the flashcard creation assignment, and persuading them to adopt new approaches to decision making.

Understand the research methods that are used to study judgement and decision-making processes in the laboratory and in real-world settings, and critically evaluate research studies related to the field.
Assess, use, document, and present information objectively and effectively, including synthesising information from multiple sources and presenting findings in a clear and concise manner.

Demonstrate the ability to argue for a position in the debate about cognitive processes and the methods used to investigate them, using critical thinking and persuasive communication skills developed throughout the course.

assessment_details.title: Flashcard Creation
assessment_details.task_description:
Flashcard Creation

Objective : To deepen your understanding of the key concepts, problems, and issues covered in the course by creating and sharing flashcards with your classmates.

Format : Each week, you will pick one concept, problem, or issue from the weekly topics and create four flashcards using a variety of formats such as “Define this term”, “Describe this process”, “True or false”, “Fill in the blank”, etc. The flashcards should be designed to challenge and engage your classmates, and to help them prepare for the in-class quiz.

Submission process : You will submit a total of ten sets of four flashcards during the 13-week semester, and you can choose which of the ten weeks you’d like to submit them. You will submit the flashcards using a link provided by your instructor straight after class on Wednesday, and you will have until Friday at 3pm to submit them. Your instructor will then upload them to the Brainscape website as soon as possible, so everyone can use them to study for the in-class quiz the following Wednesday.

Scoring : Each set of four flashcards is worth 1% (10% total), but if you post incorrect information on any of the flashcards, you will score zero for that particular set. Additionally, you can earn a 1% bonus mark each week for spotting errors in other people’s flashcard submissions, with a maximum of 3% for the entire course.

Notes

Each “Flashcard Creation” submission must correspond to the specific week covered in class. For example, in Week 8, flashcards cannot be submitted for Week 9 or Week 7. The deadline for each submission is Friday at 3pm and submissions received after that time will not be accepted. Given the structure of the assignment and the online nature of the piece, there will not be any options for extensions or resubmissions.

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI tools.

assessment_details.title: Weekly Quizzes
assessment_details.task_description:
The weekly quizzes in this class are designed to enhance your learning and improve retention of course material. Research has shown that repeated testing of information is more effective in promoting long-term retention compared to repeated studying alone (Roediger, Agarwal, McDaniel, McDermott, 2011). By taking these quizzes, you will be challenged to recall and apply the information you've learned, which will aid in solidifying the concepts and make them easier to recall in the future.

We have started to provide a list of assigned readings for each week. You will be tested, each week, on the content of the scientific papers, book chapters, webpages, videos, links, and the live activities. There will be 12 of these short quizzes, and they will be worth 5% each. We will take the 10 best marks from your quizzes throughout the semester, which will comprise 50% of your total mark.

Grades for your weekly quizzes will be posted on Blackboard under Grades a week or two following the quiz. If you’re missing your grade, or if you would like to view your quiz, please email the tutors.

This assessment task evaluates students' abilities, skills and knowledge without the aid of Artificial Intelligence (AI). Students are advised that the use of AI technologies to develop responses is strictly prohibited and may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

assessment_details.title: ChatGPT Assignment 1
assessment_details.task_description:
ChatGPT Assignment 1: Generating and Evaluating Examples of Concept Transfer
In this assignment, you will use ChatGPT to generate and evaluate different examples of a concept from the course on judgement and decision making. You will choose a concept that you find interesting and relevant, and provide three different scenarios that illustrate the concept in different ways. You will then ask the AI chatbot to demonstrate the concept in three different ways for each scenario, and rank and explain the choices the AI gave you. You will also identify and improve the most incorrect choice for each scenario. The purpose of this assignment is to help you improve your transfer skills, which are the ability to apply what you have learned in one context to another context. Transfer skills are important for learning and problem solving, as they allow you to use your existing knowledge in new and creative ways.

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI tools.

assessment_details.title: ChatGPT Assignment 2
assessment_details.task_description:
ChatGPT Assignment 2: Teaching How To Evaluate
In this assignment, you will choose a concept from the course on judgement and decision making, and use ChatGPT to generate and improve an essay on that concept. You will provide a clear and specific prompt for ChatGPT, and read and evaluate its response. You will then give suggestions for improvement, and ask ChatGPT to revise its essay based on your suggestions. You will repeat this process twice, until you get a final essay that you are satisfied with. You will also provide a summary of the changes and improvements that you and ChatGPT made, and reflect on your personal learning outcomes from this assignment.

The purpose of this assignment is to help you apply and evaluate a concept from the course in different contexts, using an AI chatbot as a source of examples. It is also to help you develop your critical thinking and feedback skills, and to explore the potential and limitations of AI chatbots as learning tools. You will be assessed on your ability to choose a relevant and interesting concept and prompt, to read and evaluate ChatGPT's essays, to provide relevant and helpful suggestions for improvement, to summarise and reflect on the revision process, and to write clearly and coherently.

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the University's assessment-related procedures can be found on my.UQ. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/services/exams-and-assessment )

Academic Integrity

It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Academic Integrity Modules - Support for Students

It may seem simple – don’t cheat and do reference your work. However, there are many issues involved in understanding what is meant by academic integrity and how you could be misled into engaging in an action which may be academic misconduct. The Academic Integrity Modules (AIM) will enable you to uphold academic integrity throughout your studies at UQ and beyond. From Semester 1, 2021, completion of the modules in your first semester of enrolment will be compulsory for all commencing students.

Plagiarism

The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

An application for an Extension of Assessment Due Date for medical grounds and/or other exceptional circumstances shall be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation by the date specified in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile. Students will be notified of the outcome of their request via the myRequests section of my.UQ (and an email sent to their student account).

Additional details associated with extension requests, including acceptable and unacceptable reasons, may be found at my.UQ .

Request for Deferral of Exams

A request for deferral of an exam must be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation via mySI-net > my Requests no later than five (5) calendar days after the date of the original exam. Students are encouraged to familiarise themselves with the information on deferring an exam available on my.UQ .

Feedback on Assessment

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the my.UQ web site. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/academic-progress-and-final-results/querying-result )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

Supplementary assessment

A supplementary assessment is designed to give you a second chance at achieving a passing grade for a course, should you receive a final course grade of 3 (or N subject to the Supplementary Assessment Procedures ). It isn't a repeat of an exam, or a specific assignment – it's designed to test the learning outcomes for the entire course, or specific learning outcomes tailored to you.

Supplementary assessment can take any form (e.g. oral, written, examination etc.), and the highest grade you can receive is a 4 (or P). Supplementary assessment isn't available for every course and is only granted in accordance with the Supplementary Assessment Procedures .

Please check the course profile to see if your course is fully or partially exempt from supplementary assessment. If you have any questions about supplementary assessment, or if you're unsure whether you qualify, please contact staff in your school office or faculty office.

Course grade cut-offs

Any change to grade cut-offs will be in accordance with PPL 3.10.02 Assessment – Procedures .

School of Psychology
Assessment Guidelines

Individual assignments must consist of entirely original material that has been developed solely for the current course. Electronic checks will be conducted to identify instances of plagiarism and collusion.

Every student must complete the University’s Academic Integrity Module (which provides advice regarding plagiarism and ethical assignment behaviour) before completing any assessment. The module can be found at: https://www.uq.edu.au/integrity

Extension Guidelines

The School of Psychology does not guarantee a trouble-free semester of study. The best preparation for unexpected illnesses and other disruptive events is to start your assignments early in the semester. Extensions are not a remedy for students experiencing more significant interruptions to study. Please refer to Section 5.3 of your course profile for specific instructions on applying for extensions or deferred examinations/quizzes. If applying through mysinet for a deferred examination (either mid-semester or end of semester) you must meet the deferred examination eligibility criteria and timelines available at https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/exams-and-assessment/deferring-exam

Students with chronic conditions are advised to contact a Disability Advisor, UQ Student Services http://www.uq.edu.au/student-services/disability

We strongly encourage students experiencing significant interruptions or chronic illnesses of greater than two weeks to contact UQ Student Services to seek information and/or assistance: http://www.uq.edu.au/student-services/

The School of Psychology uses the following guidelines in the consideration of extension requests. Course coordinators have discretion in deciding whether to grant an extension or not, and so if you are not sure whether you are eligible, please contact your course coordinator.

1. Applications must be lodged by the assignment due date unless there are extenuating circumstances.

2. If the basis of the application is a medical condition, applications should be accompanied by a medical certificate dated prior to the assignment due date, not a statutory declaration.

(a) The illness should be within the period two weeks prior to the advertised due date of the assignment; and

(b) If the illness occurs within less than two days before the due date, evidence of satisfactory progress must be submitted; and

(c) The extension granted should be in proportion to the period of the illness.

3. If the basis of the application is some other circumstance, it must be accompanied by a statutory declaration.

(a) The circumstances should have occurred within the period two weeks prior to the advertised due date of the assignment; and

(b) If the circumstances occur within less than two days before the due date, evidence of satisfactory progress must be submitted; and

(c) The extension granted should be in proportion to the period of disruption caused by the circumstances, and typically be no more than two weeks in duration.

4. Students may be asked to submit evidence of work completed to date. Lack of adequate progress on your assignment may result in an extension being refused.

5. Appeals against a refusal of an extension should be lodged through the grievance process outlined on my.UQ https://my.uq.edu.au/services/complaints-and-academic-appeals

Medical Certificates

It is the student's responsibility to ensure that the provider of a medical certificate is a genuine medical practitioner. If you use an online medical service, please ensure this is a genuine service (many are not). Please note that submission of fake documentation of any kind, even if you are not aware it is fake, constitutes misconduct.

School of Psychology Policy on AI and ChatGPT use in Assessment

The assessments in this course have been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence can provide only limited support and guidance. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI tools. Unless specifically advised by the Course Coordinator students are advised that the use of AI technologies to complete assessment is strictly prohibited and may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses

Students on a placement course – also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice – should refer to the University’s Work Integrated Learning and Work Experience Policy ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-work-integrated-learning-and-work-experience ) for detailed information.

Working with Children

Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/1.60.07-working-children ) to find out how to apply for a ‘blue card’.

Students with a Disability

Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety

Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Fitness to Practise

Students enrolled in programs and courses that include a practical placement component (also known as work integrated learning, clinical immersion, clinical placement, clinical practice, externship, fieldwork, industry experience, industry study, internship, practicum, teaching practice, work placement) should refer to the University policy Fitness to Practise ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.30.14-fitness-practise ) for detailed information on issues of competence and on management of concerns relating to patient and public safety.

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
In PSYC3052: Judgement & Decision Making, you’ll explore how people make decisions and evaluate their decision-making processes. This course is designed to give you a comprehensive understanding of key concepts and theories in the field of judgement and decision making, including biases and heuristics, dual-process theories, non-human “feats of intelligence,” reasoning and problem solving, insight and metacognition, and decision making under uncertainty.

Throughout the course, you’ll have the opportunity to work with large language models, like ChatGPT which is an AI system developed by OpenAI, as your partner. The AI will work through decision making scenarios based on prompts, and you’ll then work together to improve it by adding new information, clarifying points, fact-checking, and providing insightful analysis. Exercises like this will take advantage of the AI’s ability to simplify complex topics and provide a backdrop for you to demonstrate your understanding of the course material. With the integration of AI rapidly transforming the world, it’s essential for you to have a hands-on understanding of this innovative technology.

You will learn to apply theories in judgement and decision making to real-world situations, such as high-stakes decision making in fields like medicine, law, and education, and evaluate the decision-making processes involved. You will also develop strategies for improving your own decision-making abilities by avoiding common biases and heuristics and enhancing your ability to identify these biases. Furthermore, you will learn about advanced topics such as group decision making and nudging, the impact of brain training, transfer, and mental representations on expertise, and how to distinguish between fact and fiction. By the end of the course, you will be able to communicate your understanding of judgement and decision making to others and persuade them to adopt new approaches. These abilities are valuable because they can help you make better decisions in your personal and professional lives, and prepare you for various careers, including business, finance, and policymaking.

AI IN HIGHER EDUCATION: NAVIGATING THE TRANSFORMATION

The university sector is witnessing a rapid spread of artificial intelligence. The recent release of generative AI tools has brought about one of the biggest and quickest transformations in academia.

While AI excels in generating credible-sounding facts, it is prone to sharing false data, fabricated citations, and incorrect information. To produce meaningful results, it is crucial to have a sound understanding of the topic you’re examining. Proper use of this technology requires practice, and we’ll help you master it by working interactively with AI models to create more compelling and accurate text. This will aid you in your academic success and in preparing for your future. As AI tools are likely to become a part of your daily life, it’s important to learn how to use them effectively.

Our goal is to help you succeed in this class and prepare you for the real world. Assessments are designed to evaluate your understanding and application of the material, and the use of technologies such as ChatGPT may be essential in certain situations, while in others it will be considered academic misconduct. We’ll be transparent about when and how you should use AI and when you should not. For instance, you will complete half of your assessments with AI acting as your powerful co-pilot, while others will test your grasp of key concepts and theories, requiring unassisted completion.

COURSE INFORMATION

During each week of the course, you’ll start by delving into a new topic. We’ll provide readings and resources to get you started, but you’re encouraged to explore the topic further and use tools like ChatGPT and other large language models to clarify content, provide multiple examples, generate practice questions, distinguish similar concepts, summarise information, make connections between ideas, and receive extensive feedback. You can also participate in discussions with your classmates on the course subreddit, and challenge your understanding of the content through the “Brainscape” page where you'll upload practice quiz questions and test yourself on questions created by your peers.

Prior to each class session, we expect you to have familiarised yourself with the assigned topic and have done all of your preparation work. This way, when you arrive for class, we can dive straight into active engagement with the topic, such as making connections with other subjects, examining implications, conducting thought experiments, constructing arguments and counterarguments, rather than simply reiterating what you already know. We’ll spend as little class time lecturing at you as possible. Instead, we’ll have fist-pounding debates, meaningful conversations, activities and demonstrations that will fundamentally change the way you think about your own thought processes. To consolidate your learning, you'll complete a brief quiz during each class, which will provide immediate feedback on what you have grasped and what still needs further clarification.

During Semester 1, this course will be delivered in two modes: internal (face-to-face) and external (online). The face-to-face class will take place each Wednesday from 8-11am. Students enrolled in the internal mode will participate in weekly meetings to discuss papers, exchange ideas, and reflect on pre-class readings. For those enrolled in the external mode, the face-to-face sessions will be livestreamed via Zoom during the same 8-11am timeslot. The course has been designed to maximise the benefits of these face-to-face meetings; the external mode is intended for students who are unable to physically attend the sessions on campus. If you are located in Brisbane and able to attend the face-to-face classes, then we highly recommend that you to do so.

READINGS

We have carefully selected a range of scientific papers, book chapters, websites, videos, and links to aid in your understanding of each topic. Some of these resources are mandatory, while others are optional. Our aim is to provide a balanced mix of specialist articles and those that are written for a non-specialist audience. The purpose of this is to provide you with opportunities to develop your skills in interpreting scientific literature, while also promoting clear and concise communication of your thoughts.

We will certainly ask you about the readings in the weekly quizzes, but the questions will be designed to assess your comprehension of the articles, how well you can generalise the findings to topics that you haven't considered before, rather than testing how well you can regurgitate irrelevant details about a particular study. You might be asked to explain or apply the main concepts introduced in a chapter, interpret or explain graphs from a journal article, outline the implications of experimental findings, summarise the main points of a paper, or be asked some very basic questions about a paper to simply assess whether you've read and understand it.

ASSESSMENT

There is ample evidence to suggest that spacing your practice, testing yourself frequently, and applying the content to new situations leads to better learning and improved comprehension. With this in mind, we have devised the following assessment plan:

Weekly Quizzes (12 quizzes worth 5% each, and your lowest 2 grades will be discarded)
50% total

ChatGPT Assignment 1: Generating and Evaluating Examples (based on Part 1 of the course content)
20%

ChatGPT Assignment 2: Teaching How To Evaluate (based on Part 2 of the course content)
20%

Flashcard Creation
1% each week (10% total), with no marks awarded for posting incorrect information and up to 3% bonus marks for spotting errors in other people's flashcards

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

The course has been updated to provide an opportunity for students to work with large language models, like ChatGPT, in decision-making scenarios. These exercises allow students to improve the AI’s understanding by adding new information, fact-checking, and providing analysis, taking advantage of the AI’s ability to simplify complex topics. The new ChatGPT assignments and flashcard creation assignment aim to improve transfer skills, critical thinking, and feedback skills, and to deepen students’ understanding of course concepts.

graduate_attributes.intro: Successfully completing this course will contribute to the recognition of your attainment of the following UQ (Undergrad Pass) graduate attributes:

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A1 . A comprehensive and well-founded knowledge in the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A4 . An understanding of how other disciplines relate to the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A5 . An international perspective on the field of study.
B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .
B4 . The ability to engage effectively and appropriately with information and communication technologies .
C1 . The ability to work and learn independently .
C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.
C4 . The ability to identify problems , create solutions , innovate and improve current practices .
D1 . The ability to define and analyse problems .
D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D3 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions.
E1 . An understanding of social and civic responsibility .
E2 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .

course_grading:
Example criteria for each of the grades can be found in PPL 3.10.02 Assessment Procedures - section 7 Appendix.

Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Low Fail: Absence of evidence of achievement of course learning outcomes:

Mark = 0-23.99%

Grade 2, Fail: Minimal evidence of achievement of course learning outcomes:

Mark = 24-46.99%

Grade 3, Marginal Fail: Demonstrated evidence of developing achievement of course learning outcomes:

Mark = 47-49.99%

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrated evidence of functional achievement of course learning outcomes:

Mark = 50-64.99%

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrated evidence of proficient achievement of course learning outcomes:

Mark = 65-74.99%

Grade 6, Distinction: Demonstrated evidence of advanced achievement of course learning outcomes:

Mark = 75-84.99%

Grade 7, High Distinction: Demonstrated evidence of exceptional achievement of course learning outcomes:

Mark = 85-100%
late_submission:
Before applying for an extension please read the School of Psychology Assessment Guidelines in Section 6.1 of this Course Profile.

The School of Psychology does not guarantee a trouble-free semester of study. The best preparation for unexpected illnesses and other disruptive events is to start your assignments early in the semester. Extensions are not a remedy for students experiencing more significant interruptions to study.

Late assignments will be penalised 1 percent of your overall mark for that assignment for every day or part thereof past the submission deadline. For example, Paper 1 is worth 17% of your final mark. If you submit it 2 days late, then the most you can get for that paper is 15%.

Type of Assessment
Appropriate Pathway
Link

Assignments

Applications for extension should be submitted before the due date.

Applications for extension after the due date will only be accepted in exceptional cases (e.g. severe illness, hospitalization, or for compassionate reasons). Please refer to the policy and guidelines (6.1 of this course profile) for further information as well as the following link on my.UQ for extension eligibility https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/exams-and-assessment/applying-extension
https://my.uq.edu.au/node/218/2#2

In-Class Quizzes

Only the best 10 out of 12 Quizzes will be counted toward your final Grade. If you miss a quiz for medical reasons, your overall mark for quizzes may be reweighed. For example, if you miss one quiz due to illness, it won’t matter because we will end up dropping that quiz because it will be your lowest mark (0 out of 5). If you miss two quizzes due to illness, and you provide adequate documentation, then your final mark will be out of nine quizzes instead of ten (i.e., worth 5.56% each). If you miss three quizzes due to illness, and provide adequate documentation, then your final mark will be out of eight quizzes instead of ten (i.e., worth 6.25% each). Three or more absences will be assessed on a case-by-case basis.

The application for a deferred quiz is to be submitted to the University via mysinet>My Requests. Information regarding deferred examinations (quizzes) and details about the application process can be accessed via My.UQ

https://my.uq.edu.au/node/189/3#3

PLEASE SELECT ‘MID SEMESTER EXAM’ WHEN APPLYING FOR IN-CLASS QUIZZES
other_assessment_information:
Supplementary Assessment

Should you fail a course with a grade of 3, or a non-graded ‘N’, you may be eligible for supplementary assessment. Refer to my.UQ for information on supplementary assessment.

Supplementary assessment may not be available for all courses, or for some of the assessment items for a course. The highest grade you can receive following supplementary assessment is a 4 or P. Details of availability of supplementary assessment for this course are set out below.

Supplementary Assessment is available for this Course .

Refer to my.UQ for how to apply for supplementary assessment.

Supplementary assessment can take any form, for example, an oral or a written exam. Students who are eligible and approved for a supplementary assessment and the form of assessment is an examination, are expected to be available to sit the supplementary exam during the University’s Deferred and Supplementary examination period. Once approved, supplementary assessment cannot be rescinded by the student.
